data.title: Newell Brands prices $1.25 billion upsized senior notes offering
data.description:
• Newell Brands (NASDAQ:NWL) announced the upsizing and pricing of $1.25 billion aggregate principal amounts of 8.50% senior unsecured notes due 2028.

data.title: Turtle Beach projects $340M-$360M revenue in 2025 amid tariff challenges and new product launches
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Turtle Beach Corporation (TBCH) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Cris Keirn highlighted a 14% year-over-year revenue growth to $63.9 million in Q1 2025, driven by the full contribution of the PDP acquisition and margin improvement initiatives. Adjusted EBITDA rose to $4.1 million from $1.4 million the previous year.
  • Gross margins improved by nearly 470 basis points year-over-year, reaching 36.6%, supported by reduced promotional spending and the performance of next-generation headsets like the Stealth wireless models.
  • The company has proactively addressed tariff impacts by relocating the majority of production out of China, with less than 10% of U.S. supply now produced there, shifting most production to Vietnam. CEO Keirn emphasized that this move has positioned the company to manage tariff risks effectively.
  • CFO Mark Weinswig reported strong operational improvements, including a 180% increase in adjusted EBITDA year-over-year. Operating expenses were reduced to 34% of revenue from 42% the prior year.
  • The company authorized a new $75 million share repurchase program over the next two years, reflecting confidence in its future growth.

Outlook

  • Updated 2025 revenue guidance is between $340 million and $360 million, with adjusted EBITDA projected at $47 million to $53 million.
  • Management expects the second half of 2025 to account for two-thirds of annual revenue, driven by the launch of the Nintendo Switch 2 and other major gaming releases.
  • Despite the delay of Grand Theft Auto VI to 2026, the company anticipates improving market conditions through 2025, supported by new product launches and sustained gamer engagement.
  • Tariff-related pricing adjustments have been implemented, and further adjustments will be made if tariff conditions change.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q1 2025 was reported as $63.9 million, reflecting a 14% increase year-over-year, primarily due to the PDP acquisition.
  • Gross margin expanded to 36.6%, supported by reduced promotional activity and cost benefits from next-generation products.
  • Cash flow from operations exceeded $40 million during the quarter, marking one of the highest cash-generating quarters in the company’s history.
  • Net debt stood at $43.6 million, with $55.2 million in outstanding debt and $11.7 million in cash.

Q&A

  • Anthony Stoss, Craig-Hallum, raised concerns about the $50 million revenue revision and its drivers. CEO Keirn explained it was due to multiple factors including a sharper-than-expected decline in the U.S. gaming accessories market and broader macroeconomic challenges.
  • Sean McGowan, ROTH Capital, questioned the impact of tariffs on margins and production flexibility. CEO Keirn assured that the company’s proactive inventory and production adjustments have mitigated risks, with current projections based on existing tariff conditions.
  • Martin Yang, Oppenheimer, inquired about pricing adjustments and supply chain agility. CEO Keirn confirmed that pricing changes have already been communicated and that the company’s swift production transition is a competitive advantage.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed skepticism regarding the significant revenue guidance reduction and the broader market challenges, with concerns about tariff impacts and market softness.
  • Management maintained confidence, emphasizing robust operational adjustments and proactive strategies to mitigate external risks, though acknowledging uncertainties in the dynamic environment.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained consistent, focusing on resilience and adaptability amid market challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was revised downward for 2025, from $395 million-$405 million to $340 million-$360 million in revenue, reflecting a shift in market expectations and the delayed GTA VI release.
  • Strategic focus has shifted towards mitigating tariff impacts, with production largely moved out of China to Vietnam.
  • Analysts' tone was more pressing this quarter, with a focus on understanding the guidance reduction and tariff strategies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariffs and their impact on cost and pricing remain a significant challenge. Management’s mitigation strategies include relocating production and adjusting pricing.
  • The delay of GTA VI to 2026 could affect demand for gaming accessories in 2025.
  • Broader economic uncertainties and a declining U.S. gaming accessories market add complexity to forecasting.

Final Takeaway

Turtle Beach Corporation delivered strong Q1 2025 results, demonstrating resilience with significant revenue and EBITDA growth despite a declining market. Proactive measures against tariffs, including shifting production to Vietnam, position the company to navigate external challenges effectively. Updated guidance reflects cautious optimism, with a focus on the second half of the year driven by new product launches like the Nintendo Switch 2. Shareholder confidence is reinforced through a $75 million buyback program, underscoring long-term growth potential despite near-term headwinds.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Asian markets mixed amidst hope of U.S. China trade talks
data.description:
Asian indexes remained mixed on Friday, as investors hope for weekend talks between the U.S. and China.

Japan (NKY:IND) rose 1.49% to above 37,480. Japan’s leading economic indicators index declined to 107.7 in March 2025 from an upwardly revised 108.2 in the previous month.

Japan's index of coincident economic indicators, which tracks factory output, employment, and retail sales, fell to 116.0 in March 2025 from 117.3 in the previous month, marking the lowest reading since last November, flash data showed.

China (SHCOMP) down 0.26% to around 3,343, despite Trump hinting potential relief to current tariffs. China's exports jump 8% in April despite tariff tensions. Exports to the United States dropped more than 20%, however, as President Donald Trump's steep tariff increases took effect.

Top U.S. and Chinese trade officials are due to meet Saturday in Geneva, Switzerland. When asked on Thursday whether Trump would consider lowering tariffs on Chinese imports if this weekend’s talks go well, he said, “It could be. We’re going to see. Right now, you can’t get any higher. It’s at 145%. So we know it’s coming down.” He also said he expects the talks in Switzerland to be “substantive.”

Hong Kong (HSI) rose 0.01% to 22,777, extending gains for a seventh straight session and hovering around a one-month high.

Malaysia’s construction activity grew 16.6% Y/Y in Q1. The unemployment rate in Malaysia declined to 3.1% in March.

Indonesia's consumer confidence increased to 121.7 in April.

India (SENSEX) dropped 0.99% to 79,541 amid the ongoing war with Pakistan. The situation remains tense between the two nations.

Australia (AS51) rose 0.53% toward 8,235. The Australian dollar fell below $0.64 on Friday, hitting a one-week low as the US dollar strengthened amid improving global trade sentiment and reduced expectations of near-term US rate cuts

Wall Street on Thursday rose after President Donald Trump exhorted traders to "go out and buy stock now" following the announcement of a trade deal between the U.S. and the United Kingdom - the first of its kind since Trump's imposition of sweeping reciprocal tariffs in April.

U.S. stock futures traded in the green on Friday amidst hopes of a better than earlier expected trade scenario: Dow +0.01%; S&P 500 +0.07%; Nasdaq +0.11%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

data.title: China's exports jump 8% in April despite tariff tensions
data.description:
• China's Trade Balance for April, in Chinese Yuan (CNY) terms, came in at CNY689.99 billion, showing a slight cooling off from the previous figure of CNY736.72 billion
  • The difference was largely driven by an 8.1% Y/Y rise in exports, well above market forecasts of a 1.9% gain, despite easing from a 12.4% surge in March, as shipments to the US were dampened by Trump's tariffs.
  • Meanwhile, imports fell by 0.2%, softer than market expectations of a 5.9% decline, following a 4.3% drop in March.
  • The trade surplus with the U.S. narrowed to $20.46B in April from $27.58B in March. For the first four months of the year, the country posted a $368.8B surplus. During the period, the trade surplus with the U.S. reached $97.03B, with exports and imports shrinking 2.5% and 4.7%, respectively.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NYSEARCA:KWEB), (NYSEARCA:CQQQ), (NASDAQ:MCHI), (NYSEARCA:ASHR), (NYSEARCA:YINN), (NYSE:TDF), (NYSEARCA:CHIQ), (NYSEARCA:GXC), (NYSEARCA:EWH), (NYSEARCA:KBA), (NYSEARCA:YANG), (NASDAQ:CXSE), (NYSE:CAF), (NYSEARCA:CWEB), (NASDAQ:PGJ), (NYSEARCA:KURE).
